agent1_snippets.title: Cryptococcal Meningitis -- Etiology
agent1_snippets.content: C. neoformans is an encapsulated yeast that can be found in aged pigeon droppings which causes mild infections, from airway colonization or asymptomatic ones in laboratory workers to severe infections like meningitis or disseminated disease. It is the most common species from the Cryptococcus genre in our region (Brazil) and other temperate weathers around the world. [3] The major predisposing factor to cryptococcal infection is HIV; however, the predisposing factors in non-HIV patients are as follows [4] :

The outlook for these patients is guarded and depends on the cause of immunosuppression, extent and other comorbidity. [10] (Level V)

agent1_snippets.content: Antifungal drugs represent a pharmacologically diverse group of drugs that are crucial components in the modern medical management of mycoses. While antimycotic pharmacology has advanced significantly, particularly over the last 3 decades, common invasive fungal infections still carry a high mortality rate: Candida albicans (20% to 40% mortality), Aspergillus fumigatus (50% to 90%), and Cryptococcus neoformans (20% to 70%). agent1_snippets.title: Cutaneous Cryptococcus -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: The polysaccharide capsule antigen is a large molecule, and assays have been developed to detect the antigen in serum and other body fluids. If cryptococcal polysaccharide antigen (CA) is present in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), then there is a good chance that the yeast is present as well. The cryptococcal polysaccharide antigen assay is nearly 100% sensitive and 96% to 99.5% specific in serum and 96% to 100% sensitive and 93.5% to 99.8% specific in CSF.

agent1_snippets.title: Cryptococcus -- Epidemiology
agent1_snippets.content: Globally, approximately 1 million cases of cryptococcosis are reported each year, resulting in approximately 625,000 deaths. [2] In the United States, the incidence of cryptococcosis is estimated to be about 0.4-1.3 cases per 100,000 population and 2-7 cases per 100,000 in people affected with AIDS, with a case fatality ratio of about 12%. [3] The incidence of cryptococcal infections has declined drastically over the last two decades owing to advances in anti-retroviral therapy. Cryptococcus neoformans is usually associated with infections in immunocompromised patients, while Cryptococcus gatti is associated with infections in immunocompetent patients.

agent1_snippets.title: Cryptococcal Meningitis -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Cryptococcosis is an important opportunistic infection that causes more than 100,000 HIV-related deaths each year. It was named the Busse-Buschke disease because of its first description by Otto Busse and Abraham Buschke in 1894. [1]

agent1_snippets.title: Cryptococcal Meningitis -- Pathophysiology
agent1_snippets.content: The organism is acquired by inhalation. After being deposited into the pulmonary alveoli, the yeast spores must survive the normal to high pH and the physiological concentrations of carbon dioxide before they are phagocytized by alveolar macrophages, a more acidic environment, and disseminated after a latent period of containment in the lung lymph nodes. The essential factor in the survival of C. neoformans in this extracellular environment is glucosylceramide synthase. [5] The host response includes both cellular and humoral components. Possibly, the eradication of Cryptococci relies on natural killer cells and antibody-dependent cell-mediated killing. [6]

agent1_snippets.title: HIV-1–Associated Opportunistic Infections -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Cryptococcus neoformans: Combination therapy of flucytosine and amphotericin B is the treatment of choice for cryptococcal meningitis. An alternative regimen of fluconazole and flucytosine are options, although it is inferior to amphoterin B. [30] Duration of combination therapy is 2 weeks, following which, after confirmation of a negative CSF culture on repeat lumbar puncture, they can be discontinued and started on fluconazole 400 mg daily for 8 weeks. Following this, the dose is reduced to 200 mg daily for 1 year. Secondary prophylaxis must be reinitiated when the CD4+ counts less than 100 cells/mm^3. [21]

agent1_snippets.title: Cutaneous Cryptococcus -- Pathophysiology
agent1_snippets.content: The polysaccharide capsule, production of melanin, and growth at high temperatures are considered virulence factors for Cryptococcus species. The initial exposure occurs via the lungs through inhalation of basidiospores (1 micrometer to 2 micrometer in length) or dehydrated yeast of appropriate size. Most immunocompetent patients do not succumb to disease unless the inoculum is overwhelming. An effective immune response is generated by alveolar macrophages, which eliminate the yeast. In immunocompromised patients, the yeast continues to divide within the macrophages, which disseminate the infection throughout the body. Skin is the third most common organ system to be involved after lungs and the central nervous system.

agent1_snippets.title: Cryptococcal Meningitis -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: Blood and CSF should be cultured for fungi and tested for cryptococcal antigen. Routine lab tests may be normal even with widespread disease. Opening pressure has to be measured at the first spinal tap, a pressure over 25 cm of water is a predictor of a poor prognosis. [4]

agent1_snippets.title: Fluconazole -- Administration -- Adult Dosing
agent1_snippets.content: In treating acute cryptococcal meningitis, the recommended dosage for fluconazole is 400 mg on the first day, and subsequent dosing is 200 mg once daily. The initial therapy should be continued for 10 to 12 weeks after the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) shows negative cultures. [7] In addition, a suppression dose of 200 mg once daily is recommended in patients with AIDS. Higher daily doses of 600 to 1000 mg have been necessary for treating some endemic fungal infections like coccidioidomycosis, especially in disseminated diseases. The Infectious Disease Society of America endorses the use of fluconazole for coccidioidomycosis. [8]

agent1_snippets.title: Cryptococcal Meningitis -- Histopathology
agent1_snippets.content: C. neoformans reproduces by budding and forms round yeast-like cells that are 3 to 6 micrometers in diameter. In the host and culture media, each cell is surrounded by a large polysaccharide capsule that has antiphagocytic properties and may be immunosuppressive. It can form smooth, convex, yellow, or tan colonies on solid media at 20 to 37 degrees C. The non-pathogenic strains do not grow at this temperature. [10]

agent1_snippets.title: Cryptococcus -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Cryptococcus is an invasive fungus, transmitted through the inhalation of spores, and causes cryptococcosis, an infection commonly associated with immunosuppressed individuals. Patients present with fever, headache, malaise, photophobia, and neck stiffness as cryptococcal meningitis sets in. This activity describes the evaluation and management of cryptococcosis and reviews the role of the interprofessional team in improving care for patients with this condition.

agent1_snippets.title: Antifungal Agents -- Indications -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Fluconazole indications include the treatment of esophageal, oropharyngeal, peritoneal, urinary tract, and vaginal candidiasis. Fluconazole may also help treat systemic fungal infections, including candidemia, candida pneumonia, and cryptococcal meningitis. Fluconazole serves as a first-line agent in prophylaxis for mycoses in allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant patients. Off-label, fluconazole has a variety of applications, including blastomycosis, empiric antifungal therapy in non-neutropenic ICU patients, Candida prophylaxis (in ICU patients with a high risk of invasive Candida spp and transplant patients), and tinea. [36]

agent1_snippets.content: Fluconazole is a member of the triazole family and a commonly used antifungal agent. Prophylaxis is also known to decrease candidiasis incidence in patients receiving bone marrow transplantation who receive radiation therapy or cytotoxic chemotherapy. [2]

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2494
agent2_snippets.content: Triazole derivatives, systemic antimycotics constitute a class of antifungal agents that are administered systemically to treat invasive fungal infections. Both itraconazole and voriconazole are members of this triazole derivative class, sharing the characteristic triazole ring structure that confers broad-spectrum activity against a variety of pathogenic fungi. Their classification as systemic antimycotics underscores their use in managing serious fungal diseases where topical therapy is insufficient, highlighting the importance of these agents in comprehensive antifungal treatment strategies.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2862
agent2_snippets.content: Imidazole and triazole derivatives, topical antifungals represent a pharmacologic class of antifungal agents that includes the specific drugs isoconazole, omoconazole, and tioconazole. All three compounds share the imidazole or triazole chemical scaffold and are formulated for topical use to treat superficial fungal infections. Their classification within the same group highlights their similar mechanisms of action, spectrum of activity, and therapeutic applications in dermatologic antifungal therapy.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2004
agent2_snippets.content: Flukonazol and Vorikonazol are both classified as members of the triazole‑derivative systemic antimycotics group. As triazole antifungal agents, they share a common chemical scaffold and are used systemically to treat invasive fungal infections by inhibiting the fungal enzyme lanosterol 14α‑demethylase, thereby disrupting ergosterol synthesis and compromising fungal cell membrane integrity. Their inclusion in the same pharmacologic class underscores their similar mechanisms of action, spectrum of activity, and therapeutic role in managing systemic mycoses.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1574
agent2_snippets.content: ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE and antifungals for systemic use represent two distinct therapeutic categories within the broader class of antifungal agents. Antifungals for systemic use are employed when fungal infections require absorption and distribution throughout the body, whereas dermatological antifungals are applied topically to treat skin‑limited infections. The systemic antifungal category includes specific agents such as terbinafin, which is a member of the systemic antifungal group. Although both categories share the common goal of eradicating fungal pathogens, they differ in route of administration, site of action, and clinical indications. Recognizing the distinction between dermatological and systemic antifungal therapies is essential for selecting the appropriate treatment strategy and optimizing patient outcomes.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 621
agent2_snippets.content: Itraconazole, fluconazole, and voriconazole are members of the class Triazole derivatives, systemic antimycotics. As triazole‑based antifungal agents, they share a common mechanism of inhibiting the fungal cytochrome P450 enzyme lanosterol 14α‑demethylase, thereby disrupting ergosterol synthesis and compromising fungal cell membrane integrity. Because of this shared pharmacologic action, these drugs are employed for the treatment of a broad spectrum of systemic fungal infections, including candidiasis, aspergillosis, and other invasive mycoses.
